Keep it separate from current-turn retrieval.',
59232:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:4350:Public’s side is now concrete enough for sharper contrasts: it already has passive auto-surfacing at tool-dispatch and compaction, composite recovery via `session_bootstrap`, and governed scope fields down to `tenantId/userId/agentId/sharedSpaceId`. I’m doing one last pass over the external folder structure and a few targeted Public files so the final findings stay anchored on agent integration rather than drifting back into generic retrieval or lifecycle comparisons.
59233:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5002:   208	    // Inject memory instructions into compaction so they survive
59235:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5053:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, the repo is basically one plugin entrypoint plus package metadata: it resolves `directory || worktree || cwd`, shells out through `Bun.spawn(["mnemosyne", ...args])`, and returns five tools plus one compaction hook. There is no runtime-specific orchestration layer, no extra policy engine, and no structured adapter boundary beyond raw CLI calls.
59237:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5060:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, storing only happens when the agent explicitly calls `memory_store` or `memory_store_global`; the plugin itself never auto-captures decisions. The only passive behavior is reminder text in the compaction hook, reinforced by README-recommended `AGENTS.md` instructions. Public already goes further with session priming plus auto-surfacing at tool dispatch and compaction.
59240:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5074:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, the project collection is auto-initialized on plugin load, while the global collection is initialized only on first global write. That asymmetry makes project memory frictionless and global memory explicit. Public already supports broader local/global retrieval semantics through `retrievalLevel: "local" | "global" | "auto"`, but not via a similarly simple project/global tool pair.
59241:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5079:### Finding 5: Mnemosyne’s strongest agent-integration idea is a repeated memory-discipline checklist across AGENTS and compaction
59242:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5081:- **What it does**: Both wrapper-confirmed and README-documented, the same behavioral rules show up twice: search when history matters, store concise decisions, delete contradictions, reserve `core` for always-needed facts, and use global only for cross-project preferences. Public already injects startup and compaction notes, but those notes are more about recovery and structural context than a short memory-discipline checklist.
59243:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5086:### Finding 6: Mnemosyne’s hybrid search story stays behind the backend contract, so Public should keep memory retrieval and semantic code search separate
59245:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5111:- Questions addressed: agent-agnostic plugin shape, passive capture vs manual capture, multi-agent safety, project/global scoping ergonomics, AGENTS guidance, compaction survival, hybrid-search boundary, CocoIndex separation, offline/local integration assumptions.
59246:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5112:- Questions answered: Mnemosyne’s wrapper is intentionally minimal and portable, but it delegates nearly all safe behavior to agent discipline; it has no passive capture or actor-scoped governance, its best transferable idea is the repeated memory-discipline checklist across AGENTS and compaction, and its project-first/global-lazy ergonomics are useful even though the underlying scope model is too weak for Public.
59248:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5120:Trace the adapter contract for an external-binary memory facade that preserves Public-grade multi-agent safety: required structured response envelope, actor/session scope propagation, project-first/global escalation rules, mutation confirmation semantics, and a shared memory-discipline checklist injected at both startup and compaction.
59250:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5135:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, the repo is basically one plugin entrypoint plus package metadata: it resolves `directory || worktree || cwd`, shells out through `Bun.spawn(["mnemosyne", ...args])`, and returns five tools plus one compaction hook. There is no runtime-specific orchestration layer, no extra policy engine, and no structured adapter boundary beyond raw CLI calls.
59252:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5142:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, storing only happens when the agent explicitly calls `memory_store` or `memory_store_global`; the plugin itself never auto-captures decisions. The only passive behavior is reminder text in the compaction hook, reinforced by README-recommended `AGENTS.md` instructions. Public already goes further with session priming plus auto-surfacing at tool dispatch and compaction.
59255:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5156:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, the project collection is auto-initialized on plugin load, while the global collection is initialized only on first global write. That asymmetry makes project memory frictionless and global memory explicit. Public already supports broader local/global retrieval semantics through `retrievalLevel: "local" | "global" | "auto"`, but not via a similarly simple project/global tool pair.
59256:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5161:### Finding 5: Mnemosyne’s strongest agent-integration idea is a repeated memory-discipline checklist across AGENTS and compaction
59257:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5163:- **What it does**: Both wrapper-confirmed and README-documented, the same behavioral rules show up twice: search when history matters, store concise decisions, delete contradictions, reserve `core` for always-needed facts, and use global only for cross-project preferences. Public already injects startup and compaction notes, but those notes are more about recovery and structural context than a short memory-discipline checklist.
59258:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5168:### Finding 6: Mnemosyne’s hybrid search story stays behind the backend contract, so Public should keep memory retrieval and semantic code search separate
59260:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5193:- Questions addressed: agent-agnostic plugin shape, passive capture vs manual capture, multi-agent safety, project/global scoping ergonomics, AGENTS guidance, compaction survival, hybrid-search boundary, CocoIndex separation, offline/local integration assumptions.
59261:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5194:- Questions answered: Mnemosyne’s wrapper is intentionally minimal and portable, but it delegates nearly all safe behavior to agent discipline; it has no passive capture or actor-scoped governance, its best transferable idea is the repeated memory-discipline checklist across AGENTS and compaction, and its project-first/global-lazy ergonomics are useful even though the underlying scope model is too weak for Public.
59263:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-007.md:5202:Trace the adapter contract for an external-binary memory facade that preserves Public-grade multi-agent safety: required structured response envelope, actor/session scope propagation, project-first/global escalation rules, mutation confirmation semantics, and a shared memory-discipline checklist injected at both startup and compaction.
59267:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:13:- **Why it matters for us**: we should reject any conclusion that Mnemosyne introduces hybrid retrieval as a net-new capability for Public. The real novelty is wrapper shape, scoping ergonomics, and compaction guidance.
59268:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:17:### Finding 5: The compaction hook is the strongest transferable idea because it preserves tool awareness, not just context payload
59269:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:20:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne pushes a static instruction block into `output.context` during `experimental.session.compacting`, explicitly naming `memory_recall`, `memory_store`, `memory_delete`, and the global variants. Public already preserves structural context through the compact-code-graph transport and can prepend surfaced constitutional/triggered memories during compaction, but that path does not add the same blunt “you still have these memory tools, and here is when to use them” reminder.
59270:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:21:- **Why it matters for us**: this is a low-cost, high-leverage addition for compaction resilience. Structural continuity and surfaced memories help, but direct tool reminders reduce the chance that the agent forgets to recall/store/delete after a context reset.
59273:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:29:- **Why it matters for us**: the discipline pattern is worth adopting in Public prompts and compaction guidance, but merging memory retrieval with semantic code search would blur two distinct object types: human/project memory versus code discovery.
59274:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:48:- Questions addressed: hybrid retrieval boundary, model/runtime implications, project-vs-global scoping, compaction survival, AGENTS guidance, CocoIndex separation, plugin-vs-MCP adoption shape, offline-first tradeoffs
59275:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:49:- Questions answered: the wrapper is thin; hybrid ranking is README-level not wrapper-owned; Public already has deeper hybrid retrieval; project/global split is useful but collision-prone; compaction tool reminders are the best adopt-now idea; CocoIndex should stay separate from memory retrieval
59276:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:54:- What worked: reading in the mandated order exposed the true architecture quickly; comparing wrapper code against Public’s live hybrid and compaction code prevented stale conclusions
59278:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:58:Investigate lifecycle ergonomics in more detail: document ID flow from recall to delete, basename-collision risks for project scoping, whether Public should add a dedicated memory-tool compaction reminder block, and whether an optional OpenCode memory plugin facade is worth prototyping on top of the existing MCP-backed memory system.
59279:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:65:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
59280:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:69:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
59281:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:77:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
59283:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:90:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
59284:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:92:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
59286:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:103:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
59287:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:104:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
59288:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:106:11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
59289:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:107:12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.
59290:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:114:    Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/opencode-mnemosyne-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around hybrid search (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin architecture, memory scoping (project vs global), session compaction resilience, and offline-first memory design.
59292:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:126:4. What exactly does the compaction hook preserve, and how does prompt-level tool guidance differ from Public's current structural-context compaction transport?
59295:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:140:- Do examine the compaction hook as the phase's highest-priority differentiator for `Code_Environment/Public`.
59296:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:141:- Do compare against current Public files so the research reflects today's hybrid retrieval and compaction behavior.
59297:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:148:- Do not ignore the compaction hook; it is the most novel and transferable feature for `Code_Environment/Public`.
59298:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:171:**Finding: Memory-tool awareness survives compaction through prompt injection**
59299:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:173:- What it does: Mnemosyne appends a compact memory-usage instruction block during `experimental.session.compacting`, while Public's existing compaction plugin injects structural transport context rather than memory-tool reminders.
59300:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:174:- Why it matters: Public may already preserve structural state across compaction, but it does not yet remind the agent of concrete memory tools in the same direct way.

Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
59377:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-003.md:72:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
59379:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-003.md:85:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
59380:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-003.md:87:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
59382:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/iterations/iteration-003.md:98:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. 60421:I checked the phase folder too: there are earlier iteration artifacts under `research/iterations/`, so I’m deliberately steering away from the already-covered wrapper-thinness and generic compaction points. This pass is narrowing to lifecycle ownership: who mints state, who verifies readiness, how context is resumed, and how session knowledge gets serialized for the next run.
60486:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne trims the working path, derives a project name from the basename, then eagerly runs `mnemosyne init --name <project>` during plugin load at lines 27-32 and 78-90. After that, it exposes only five tools plus a compaction hook. Public’s lifecycle is materially different: `session_bootstrap()` composes `session_resume()`, `session_health()`, structural context, payload contracts, graph ops, hints, and `nextActions` in one typed recovery surface at lines 163-340, while `session_resume()` separately reports memory, graph, and CocoIndex readiness at lines 400-507.
60498:### Finding 3: Mnemosyne survives compaction with reminders; Public resumes with actual session state
60500:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s `experimental.session.compacting` hook at lines 208-223 injects a static checklist telling the agent when to recall, store, delete, or use `core` and `global`. Public’s compaction transport at `spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` lines 396-416 injects a prebuilt transport block, and `memory_context()` can inject actual prior session prompt context before budget enforcement at lines 1425-1445. `session_bootstrap()` then layers structural hints and routing nudges on top at lines 194-208.
60514:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne derives project scope from `path.basename(targetDir)` and rewrites a literal `global` basename to `default` at lines 27-30; the README confirms project memory is keyed to `<directory-name>` and global memory is separate at lines 51-60. Public’s save path instead treats the explicit CLI spec folder as authoritative at `generate-context.js` lines 85-93 and 338-345, while `context-server.ts` lines 764-789 publishes a session recovery digest around spec folder, graph freshness, and recommended next step.
60515:- **Why it matters for us**: Mnemosyne’s ergonomics are nice, but basename keying is a weak lifecycle anchor. Renaming a repo or opening another checkout silently changes the memory namespace. If we ever add project/global aliases, they should sit on top of canonical spec-folder or logical identity keys.
60521:- **What it does**: The checked-out Mnemosyne wrapper exposes tool calls and a static compaction block, but no cached continuity summary, transcript fingerprint, token reuse note, or recovery digest. Public adds accepted cached continuity into `session_resume()` at lines 479-488, carries it through `session_bootstrap()` at lines 202-205 and 255-266, and exposes a session recovery digest with recommended action in `context-server.ts` lines 764-789.
60542:- Questions addressed: bootstrap/readiness ownership, trusted session identity, compaction versus actual resume state, structured context save semantics, namespace stability across project lifecycle changes, cached continuity versus raw recall.
60543:- Questions answered: Mnemosyne’s lifecycle is storage-first and instruction-driven; Public’s lifecycle is session-first and contract-driven. The strongest reusable ideas are lightweight project/global ergonomics and optional compaction reminders, but Mnemosyne’s stateless tool boundary, basename-derived identity, and string-only session save model should not replace `memory_context`, `generate-context.js`, or `session_bootstrap`. The exact strict validation command passed with `RESULT: PASSED` and `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0`, alongside the expected read-only warning `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.

60690:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
60694:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
60700:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
60702:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
60715:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
60717:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
60728:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
60729:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
60731:11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
60732:12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.

60796:**Finding: Memory-tool awareness survives compaction through prompt injection**

60799:- Why it matters: Public may already preserve structural state across compaction, but it does not yet remind the agent of concrete memory tools in the same direct way.
60801:- Affected area: compaction strategy, startup guidance, memory-tool ergonomics
60821:- project/global scoping model
60822:- documented hybrid search contract
60824:- compaction-hook behavior and comparison with Public
60825:- offline-first operational tradeoffs
60837:Rank findings in this order: compaction-resilience leverage, fit with Public's current hybrid retrieval stack, clarity of wrapper-vs-backend boundary, usefulness of project/global scoping ergonomics, offline-first operational value, implementation cost, and clean differentiation from phases `001` and `003`.

61233:OpenCode plugin for **local persistent memory** using [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne). Gives your AI coding agent memory that persists across sessions -- entirely offline, no cloud APIs.
61235:This is the local/offline alternative to cloud-based memory plugins like opencode-supermemory.
61248:See the [mnemosyne README](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne#quick-start) for detailed setup instructions. On first use, mnemosyne will automatically download its ML models (~500 MB one-time).

66237:   139	The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.

66781:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, plus README-documented: the wrapper injects a short “search/store/delete/core/global” checklist during compaction, and the README recommends pasting a matching AGENTS block into projects. The published package exposes only the plugin wrapper surface, not separate validation, repair, or hygiene modules.
66782:- **Why it matters for us**: Public already enforces hygiene in runtime tools; Mnemosyne mainly reminds the agent to behave well. The reusable part is the lightweight hygiene reminder, not the instruction-only governance model. Affected subsystem: compaction/startup guidance.
66795:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed: Mnemosyne exposes semantic recall for project/global stores, but nothing like trigger phrases, prompt-start matching, session-scoped activation, co-activation, or tiered content trimming. Public’s `memory_match_triggers` validates trusted session IDs, filters by scope, runs attention decay/co-activation, and limits by cognitive state.
66809:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed: the plugin silently auto-initializes project/global collections and returns a friendly install string when the binary is missing. README-documented: first use downloads a large local model. Public’s `memory_health` explicitly reports DB/model/vector/FTS/alias/orphan status and gates repair behind `confirmed:true`.
66838:- Questions addressed: hygiene enforcement versus guidance, trigger-based recall, mutation safety, contradiction handling, operational health/repair, packaging boundary and external repo thinness, AGENTS guidance location, offline-first readiness cost.
66839:- Questions answered: Mnemosyne’s memory hygiene is wrapper-thin and agent-disciplined; Public’s hygiene is system-disciplined through save quality gates, trigger recall, causal lineage, mutation ledgers, and repair tooling. The best transferable idea is a short Mnemosyne-style hygiene reminder layered onto our existing compaction/startup surfaces, not any simplification of our save/delete/health/trigger contracts. The exact strict validation command passed with `RESULT: PASSED` and `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0`, while also emitting the expected read-only warning `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.
66861:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed, plus README-documented: the wrapper injects a short “search/store/delete/core/global” checklist during compaction, and the README recommends pasting a matching AGENTS block into projects. The published package exposes only the plugin wrapper surface, not separate validation, repair, or hygiene modules.
66862:- **Why it matters for us**: Public already enforces hygiene in runtime tools; Mnemosyne mainly reminds the agent to behave well. The reusable part is the lightweight hygiene reminder, not the instruction-only governance model. Affected subsystem: compaction/startup guidance.
66875:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed: Mnemosyne exposes semantic recall for project/global stores, but nothing like trigger phrases, prompt-start matching, session-scoped activation, co-activation, or tiered content trimming. Public’s `memory_match_triggers` validates trusted session IDs, filters by scope, runs attention decay/co-activation, and limits by cognitive state.
66889:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed: the plugin silently auto-initializes project/global collections and returns a friendly install string when the binary is missing. README-documented: first use downloads a large local model. Public’s `memory_health` explicitly reports DB/model/vector/FTS/alias/orphan status and gates repair behind `confirmed:true`.
66918:- Questions addressed: hygiene enforcement versus guidance, trigger-based recall, mutation safety, contradiction handling, operational health/repair, packaging boundary and external repo thinness, AGENTS guidance location, offline-first readiness cost.
66919:- Questions answered: Mnemosyne’s memory hygiene is wrapper-thin and agent-disciplined; Public’s hygiene is system-disciplined through save quality gates, trigger recall, causal lineage, mutation ledgers, and repair tooling. The best transferable idea is a short Mnemosyne-style hygiene reminder layered onto our existing compaction/startup surfaces, not any simplification of our save/delete/health/trigger contracts. 66983:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
66987:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
66993:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
66995:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
67008:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
67010:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
67021:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
67022:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. 67519:OpenCode plugin for **local persistent memory** using [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne). Gives your AI coding agent memory that persists across sessions -- entirely offline, no cloud APIs.
67521:This is the local/offline alternative to cloud-based memory plugins like opencode-supermemory.
67534:See the [mnemosyne README](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne#quick-start) for detailed setup instructions. On first use, mnemosyne will automatically download its ML models (~500 MB one-time).

68620:The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.
68920:      const block = plan?.compaction;
69346:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md:240:**Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** combines all channel results using the formula `1/(K + rank)`. The K parameter is tuned per query intent through sensitivity analysis across K values {10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120}. A memory that scores well in multiple channels rises to the top because RRF gives exponential weight to high-ranking items while still including lower-ranked contributions.

79857:### Finding 1: Add a compaction-time memory action card, not just a continuity note
79859:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed: Mnemosyne pushes an explicit post-compaction checklist into `output.context` telling the agent when to recall, store, delete, use `core`, and use global memory. Public’s transport/plugin path pushes a deduped continuity block, but that block is transport-only state, not a memory-operations playbook.
79860:- **Why it matters for us**: Public preserves state across compaction, but it does not re-teach memory behavior at the moment the agent most needs it. A new compact “memory action card” would complement `session_bootstrap` and structural transport instead of replacing them.
79866:- **What it does**: Both: Mnemosyne exposes separate project and global tools, with auto-init for project memory and lazy-init for global memory. Public exposes one generalized memory surface with `specFolder`, `tenantId`, `userId`, `agentId`, and `sharedSpaceId`; meanwhile its `retrievalLevel: global` is community fallback, not a personal/global preference lane.
79880:- **What it does**: Both: Mnemosyne lets an agent set `core=true` at creation time and treats that as always-relevant context. Public guarantees constitutional memories are always surfaced, but creation-time pinning is not a first-class tool parameter; it is encoded via file metadata or later `memory_update`.
79885:### Finding 5: Build an explicit offline warmup and doctor flow for memory readiness
79908:- Questions addressed: compaction-specific memory guidance, project-vs-global ergonomics, direct memory capture, write-time pinning, offline readiness, AGENTS guidance location, overlap with existing hybrid retrieval and community-search fallback.
79909:- Questions answered: Public already owns the heavy hybrid retrieval, trigger recall, health, and governance story. The real missing surface area is ergonomic: a compaction memory action card, explicit scoped lanes, inline capture, write-time pinning, and proactive warmup. Mnemosyne’s most transferable value is its thin agent-facing contract, not its ranking backend. The exact strict validation command passed with `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while also emitting the read-only warning `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`. No `AGENTS.md` file was present under the external checkout; that guidance exists only in the README.
79916:Prototype a thin Public-side memory facade with three pieces together: `memory_remember` for inline capture, a `scope`/`pin` authoring contract for project-personal-always-on memories, and a compaction memory action card injected alongside the existing transport payload. That will test whether Mnemosyne’s best idea for us is not new ranking math, but a much thinner agent-facing memory UX.

80042:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
80046:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
80052:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
80054:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
80067:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
80069:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
80080:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
80081:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
80083:11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
80084:12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.

80148:**Finding: Memory-tool awareness survives compaction through prompt injection**
80150:- What it does: Mnemosyne appends a compact memory-usage instruction block during `experimental.session.compacting`, while Public's existing compaction plugin injects structural transport context rather than memory-tool reminders.
80151:- Why it matters: Public may already preserve structural state across compaction, but it does not yet remind the agent of concrete memory tools in the same direct way.
80153:- Affected area: compaction strategy, startup guidance, memory-tool ergonomics
80173:- project/global scoping model
80174:- documented hybrid search contract
80176:- compaction-hook behavior and comparison with Public
80177:- offline-first operational tradeoffs
80189:Rank findings in this order: compaction-resilience leverage, fit with Public's current hybrid retrieval stack, clarity of wrapper-vs-backend boundary, usefulness of project/global scoping ergonomics, offline-first operational value, implementation cost, and clean differentiation from phases `001` and `003`.
80195:- explicit comparison against current Public files for hybrid retrieval, session recovery, compaction injection, and CocoIndex positioning

80593:OpenCode plugin for **local persistent memory** using [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne). Gives your AI coding agent memory that persists across sessions -- entirely offline, no cloud APIs.
80595:This is the local/offline alternative to cloud-based memory plugins like opencode-supermemory.
80608:See the [mnemosyne README](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne#quick-start) for detailed setup instructions. On first use, mnemosyne will automatically download its ML models (~500 MB one-time).

81715:139:The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.

84644:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/feature_catalog/17--governance/05-constitutional-gate-enforcement-rule-pack.md:19:In practice, this pack gives the memory system a compact "do not forget these rules" layer. The core gate-enforcement file is intentionally lean: it does not restate every gate in full, but instead keeps the most important trigger phrases, a gate cross-reference, quick-reference tables, and edge-case rules for continuation validation and compaction recovery. The companion constitutional README explains why this works operationally: constitutional memories are pinned to the top of search results, do not decay, and are designed to surface even when the user query is only loosely related.

86695:### Finding 1: Refactor compaction guidance into the existing shared transport pipeline instead of adding a second Mnemosyne-style memory hook
86697:- **What it does**: Wrapper-confirmed on Mnemosyne: compaction always injects a static memory-tool usage card. Public already has compaction-specific memory surfacing and transport injection, but its OpenCode compaction block is generic continuity payload text rather than explicit memory-tool instructions.
86698:- **Why it matters for us**: The gap is not missing compaction infrastructure; it is split messaging. The right change is to enrich the existing shared compaction payload with concise memory actions and routing cues, not bolt on a parallel plugin-local instruction block.
86709:### Finding 3: If we add scoped memory ergonomics, implement them as aliases over governed scope metadata, not as separate project/global stores
86718:- **What it does**: Both: Mnemosyne surfaces `core=true` at write time. Public already has latent pinned-memory semantics in storage and ranking (`is_pinned` bypasses decay), but `memory_save` and `memory_update` do not expose pinning in the tool contract.
86726:- **Why it matters for us**: Public should not regress toward doc-dependent behavior. If we want Mnemosyne’s proactive recall pattern, the right place is runtime-owned prime/compaction payloads so it works consistently across sessions and runtimes.
86758:- Questions addressed: compaction-hook ownership, readiness/warmup architecture, scoped-memory ergonomics, latent pinning support, AGENTS guidance role, plugin-vs-MCP architecture, CocoIndex separation.
86759:- Questions answered: The strongest refactor opportunities are all surface-level and integration-level, not ranking-backend replacements. Public already has the core primitives for compaction continuity, readiness checks, scoped governance, and pinning; the missing work is to expose and unify them more cleanly. The exact strict validation command passed with `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while still emitting `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`. No `AGENTS.md` exists under the external checkout.
86762:- What worked: Starting with the wrapper, then tracing Public’s compaction, memory-surface, governance, and readiness paths exposed which gaps are actually latent-feature refactors rather than missing systems.
86766:Prototype one narrow refactor slice first: extend the shared compaction payload so OpenCode receives explicit memory-tool continuity guidance from the existing `memory-surface` plus transport pipeline. If that works cleanly, follow with a second slice that exposes `pin` and scoped save aliases on top of the current governed `memory_save` path instead of creating parallel tools or stores.

86911:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
86915:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
86921:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
86923:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
86936:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
86938:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
86949:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
86950:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
86952:11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
86953:12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.

86972:4. What exactly does the compaction hook preserve, and how does prompt-level tool guidance differ from Public's current structural-context compaction transport?

87017:**Finding: Memory-tool awareness survives compaction through prompt injection**
87019:- What it does: Mnemosyne appends a compact memory-usage instruction block during `experimental.session.compacting`, while Public's existing compaction plugin injects structural transport context rather than memory-tool reminders.
87020:- Why it matters: Public may already preserve structural state across compaction, but it does not yet remind the agent of concrete memory tools in the same direct way.
87022:- Affected area: compaction strategy, startup guidance, memory-tool ergonomics
87042:- project/global scoping model
87043:- documented hybrid search contract
87045:- compaction-hook behavior and comparison with Public
87046:- offline-first operational tradeoffs

87703:The wrapper is as thin as the brief implied: a single TypeScript entrypoint, one plugin manifest, and no backend source beyond the CLI bridge. I’m now cross-walking the exact behaviors that are actually source-proven here, especially compaction injection, scope derivation, lazy global init, and the `core` write path against Public’s current memory surfaces.
87709:     3	OpenCode plugin for **local persistent memory** using [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne). Gives your AI coding agent memory that persists across sessions -- entirely offline, no cloud APIs.
87711:     5	This is the local/offline alternative to cloud-based memory plugins like opencode-supermemory.
87724:    18	See the [mnemosyne README](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne#quick-start) for detailed setup instructions. On first use, mnemosyne will automatically download its ML models (~500 MB one-time).

88404:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md:535:**Architecture:** CocoIndex (semantic, external MCP) finds code by concept. Code Graph (structural, this server) maps imports, calls and hierarchy. Memory (session, this server) preserves decisions. The compact-merger combines all three under a 4000-token budget for compaction injection.

91953:   139	The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.
92543:I’ve now got the critical contrast: Mnemosyne’s compaction hook is a hard-coded instruction block about when to use memory tools, while Public’s compaction transport is payload-driven structural context and dedupe-aware. Next I’m checking whether Public already has a latent path for operational guidance injection, because that determines whether the paradigm shift is “new subsystem” or just “new payload layer.”

93441:### Finding 1: Shift compaction from passive state carryover to runtime-owned behavioral continuity
93443:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne hard-codes a memory-usage instruction block into `experimental.session.compacting`, telling the agent when to recall, store, delete, use `core`, and use global memory. Public already has transport-level compaction notes and prime packages, and Claude/Gemini already inject explicit recovery instructions, but OpenCode transport is still marked `retrievalPolicyOwner: 'runtime'` and only ships continuity payloads, not explicit memory-action guidance.
93444:- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is that compaction should preserve not just facts, but the agent’s operating model for memory. Public already has the infrastructure; the gap is OpenCode-specific behavioral guidance, not a missing compaction system.
93476:### Finding 6: Make offline backend readiness a first-class memory concern, not a hidden first-use side effect
93478:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s README documents a one-time model download of about 500 MB, and the wrapper handles missing binaries with a string fallback. Its project auto-init is best-effort and does not inspect the `init` exit status before logging success. Public, by contrast, already models readiness and recovery through `session_bootstrap`, structural status, and explicit next actions.
93479:- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is to treat offline model/backend warmup as architecture, not ops trivia. If Public ever adds heavier local inference backends, readiness, degraded mode, and repair guidance should stay first-class surfaces.
93502:- Questions addressed: compaction-hook leverage, API-surface vs handler reality, operational `core` vs pinning semantics, runtime facade layering, memory-vs-code retrieval boundaries, offline-first readiness tradeoffs
93503:- Questions answered: The strongest paradigm shifts are not new ranking algorithms. They are API and runtime shifts: compaction should preserve memory behavior, retrieval posture must be explicit in the tool contract, pinning should be separated from governance tiers, OpenCode should get a curated facade over the governed MCP core, and memory retrieval should remain one plane in a federated routing stack. The exact validation command passed with `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while still emitting `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.
93510:Prototype one API-surface alignment slice instead of a backend rewrite: add an explicit OpenCode-facing retrieval facade that exposes `retrievalLevel` and `pin`, then extend the OpenCode compaction payload with bounded memory-behavior guidance so OpenCode reaches parity with the existing Claude/Gemini recovery pattern.

Public already has transport-level compaction notes and prime packages, and Claude/Gemini already inject explicit recovery instructions, but OpenCode transport is still marked `retrievalPolicyOwner: 'runtime'` and only ships continuity payloads, not explicit memory-action guidance.
93524:- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is that compaction should preserve not just facts, but the agent’s operating model for memory. Public already has the infrastructure; the gap is OpenCode-specific behavioral guidance, not a missing compaction system.

93559:- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is to treat offline model/backend warmup as architecture, not ops trivia. If Public ever adds heavier local inference backends, readiness, degraded mode, and repair guidance should stay first-class surfaces.
93582:- Questions addressed: compaction-hook leverage, API-surface vs handler reality, operational `core` vs pinning semantics, runtime facade layering, memory-vs-code retrieval boundaries, offline-first readiness tradeoffs
93583:- Questions answered: The strongest paradigm shifts are not new ranking algorithms. They are API and runtime shifts: compaction should preserve memory behavior, retrieval posture must be explicit in the tool contract, pinning should be separated from governance tiers, OpenCode should get a curated facade over the governed MCP core, and memory retrieval should remain one plane in a federated routing stack. The exact validation command passed with `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while still emitting `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.

Public, by contrast, already models readiness and recovery through `session_bootstrap`, structural status, and explicit next actions.
93624:- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is to treat offline model/backend warmup as architecture, not ops trivia. If Public ever adds heavier local inference backends, readiness, degraded mode, and repair guidance should stay first-class surfaces.
93647:- Questions addressed: compaction-hook leverage, API-surface vs handler reality, operational `core` vs pinning semantics, runtime facade layering, memory-vs-code retrieval boundaries, offline-first readiness tradeoffs
93648:- Questions answered: The strongest paradigm shifts are not new ranking algorithms. They are API and runtime shifts: compaction should preserve memory behavior, retrieval posture must be explicit in the tool contract, pinning should be separated from governance tiers, OpenCode should get a curated facade over the governed MCP core, and memory retrieval should remain one plane in a federated routing stack. The exact validation command passed with `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while still emitting `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.
93655:Prototype one API-surface alignment slice instead of a backend rewrite: add an explicit OpenCode-facing retrieval facade that exposes `retrievalLevel` and `pin`, then extend the OpenCode compaction payload with bounded memory-behavior guidance so OpenCode reaches parity with the existing Claude/Gemini recovery pattern.
93662:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
93666:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
93672:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
93674:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
93687:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
93689:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
93700:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
