98421:Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/opencode-mnemosyne-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around hybrid search (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin architecture, memory scoping (project vs global), session compaction resilience, and offline-first memory design.
98425:### Finding 1: The strongest pattern is compaction-time preservation of memory behavior, not memory content
98433:### Finding 2: Mnemosyne turns memory use into a lifecycle contract by repeating the same policy at startup and compaction
98436:- **What it does**: the README recommends putting the same recall/store/delete/global/core rules in `AGENTS.md`, while the compaction hook re-injects nearly the same operating model after context resets.
98437:- **Why it matters for us**: the insight is not “write better docs.” It is “surface retrieval posture as runtime contract.” Public already has rich recovery tools, but OpenCode currently does not restate that posture as directly during compaction.
98444:- **What it does**: project scope is derived from the working directory basename, `global` is a separate write/read path, and `core` is expressed as a simple tag. The compaction text teaches those choices in human terms rather than schema terms.
98449:### Finding 4: Public already preserves continuity across compaction, but its OpenCode surface intentionally avoids owning retrieval policy
98452:- **What it does**: Public builds an OpenCode transport plan from shared payloads, labels `retrievalPolicyOwner: 'runtime'`, and injects continuity notes during compaction. Claude and Gemini also recover cached compact briefs plus a `memory_context(...resume...)` instruction.
98453:- **Why it matters for us**: the gap is narrow and specific. We do not lack compaction support. We lack Mnemosyne-style, OpenCode-facing memory-behavior guidance on top of the continuity payload.
98460:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s wrapper is just a Bun-spawned CLI bridge plus hook text; the hybrid BM25/vector/RRF implementation is only README-documented here. Public already has hybrid retrieval, `retrievalLevel` local/global/auto, community fallback, and internal `is_pinned` ranking behavior, but those operational controls are not surfaced as clearly in the OpenCode-facing memory contract.
98481:- Questions addressed: compaction-hook payload contents, startup-vs-compaction lifecycle reinforcement, scope semantics in the wrapper, wrapper-vs-backend boundary, OpenCode transport comparison, current Public retrieval overlap
98482:- Questions answered: the single most impactful pattern is not hybrid ranking math. It is explicit memory-behavior injection at compaction time. Mnemosyne preserves agent posture by re-teaching the agent which memory tools exist and when to use them. Public already preserves continuity and recovery state, but its OpenCode path stops short of that behavioral layer.
98490:Prototype a narrowly scoped OpenCode memory-policy addendum in the existing transport path: keep `session_bootstrap` and shared payloads authoritative, but add a bounded compaction/startup behavior block that tells OpenCode when to use `memory_match_triggers`, when to call `memory_context(...resume...)`, and how to choose `memory_search` with `retrievalLevel: local/global/auto`. That would import Mnemosyne’s strongest idea without diluting Public’s governed core.
98504:Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/opencode-mnemosyne-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around hybrid search (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin architecture, memory scoping (project vs global), session compaction resilience, and offline-first memory design.

98584:### Finding 6: Make offline backend readiness a first-class memory concern, not a hidden first-use side effect
98586:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s README documents a one-time model download of about 500 MB, and the wrapper handles missing binaries with a string fallback. Its project auto-init is best-effort and does not inspect the `init` exit status before logging success. Public, by contrast, already models readiness and recovery through `session_bootstrap`, structural status, and explicit next actions.
98587:- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is to treat offline model/backend warmup as architecture, not ops trivia. If Public ever adds heavier local inference backends, readiness, degraded mode, and repair guidance should stay first-class surfaces.

98611:- Questions answered: The strongest paradigm shifts are not new ranking algorithms. They are API and runtime shifts: compaction should preserve memory behavior, retrieval posture must be explicit in the tool contract, pinning should be separated from governance tiers, OpenCode should get a curated facade over the governed MCP core, and memory retrieval should remain one plane in a federated routing stack. 98837:- project/global scoping model
98838:- documented hybrid search contract
98840:- compaction-hook behavior and comparison with Public
98841:- offline-first operational tradeoffs

98941:/bin/zsh -lc 'rg -n "mnemosyne|compaction|runtime facade|federated stack|hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade|004-opencode-mnemosyne-main" /Users/michelkerkmeester/.codex/memories/MEMORY.md' in /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public
99458:    28	### Finding 6: Make offline backend readiness a first-class memory concern, not a hidden first-use side effect
99460:    30	- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s README documents a one-time model download of about 500 MB, and the wrapper handles missing binaries with a string fallback. Its project auto-init is best-effort and does not inspect the `init` exit status before logging success. Public, by contrast, already models readiness and recovery through `session_bootstrap`, structural status, and explicit next actions.
99461:    31	- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is to treat offline model/backend warmup as architecture, not ops trivia. If Public ever adds heavier local inference backends, readiness, degraded mode, and repair guidance should stay first-class surfaces.
99484:    54	- Questions addressed: compaction-hook leverage, API-surface vs handler reality, operational `core` vs pinning semantics, runtime facade layering, memory-vs-code retrieval boundaries, offline-first readiness tradeoffs
99485:    55	- Questions answered: The strongest paradigm shifts are not new ranking algorithms. They are API and runtime shifts: compaction should preserve memory behavior, retrieval posture must be explicit in the tool contract, pinning should be separated from governance tiers, OpenCode should get a curated facade over the governed MCP core, and memory retrieval should remain one plane in a federated routing stack. The exact validation command passed with `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while still emitting `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.
99492:    62	Prototype one API-surface alignment slice instead of a backend rewrite: add an explicit OpenCode-facing retrieval facade that exposes `retrievalLevel` and `pin`, then extend the OpenCode compaction payload with bounded memory-behavior guidance so OpenCode reaches parity with the existing Claude/Gemini recovery pattern.
99499:    69	You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
99503:    73	Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
99509:    79	OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
99511:    81	The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
99524:    94	`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
99526:    96	What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
99537:   107	8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
99538:   108	9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
99540:   110	11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
99541:   111	12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.

100209:     3	OpenCode plugin for **local persistent memory** using [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne). Gives your AI coding agent memory that persists across sessions -- entirely offline, no cloud APIs.
100211:     5	This is the local/offline alternative to cloud-based memory plugins like opencode-supermemory.
100224:    18	See the [mnemosyne README](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne#quick-start) for detailed setup instructions. On first use, mnemosyne will automatically download its ML models (~500 MB one-time).
100248:    42	| `memory_recall_global` | Search global memory for cross-project preferences |
100255:    49	- **`experimental.session.compacting`** -- Injects memory tool instructions into the compaction prompt so the agent retains awareness of its memory capabilities across context window resets.
100263:    57	| Core (project) | `<directory-name>` (tagged `core`) | Sessions + survives compaction |
100264:    58	| Core (global) | `global` (tagged `core`) | All projects + survives compaction |
100280:    74	- Mark critical, always-relevant context as core (core=true) — but use sparingly.
100288:    82	Mnemosyne is a local document store with hybrid search:
100796:    77	 *   compaction?: TransportBlock,
101033:   139	The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.

102970:- **What it does**: README-documented Mnemosyne promises BM25 plus vector plus RRF, but wrapper-confirmed behavior exposes only memory tools over `mnemosyne search/add/delete`. Public, by contrast, explicitly routes memory retrieval to `memory_context` or `memory_search`, semantic code retrieval to `mcp__cocoindex_code__search`, and structural code retrieval to `code_graph_query`.
102975:### Finding 2: Public’s memory plane already goes deeper than Mnemosyne’s project/global split through `retrievalLevel` and community fallback
102984:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s README recommends AGENTS instructions and its compaction hook reminds the agent that memory tools exist. Public goes further: bootstrap, auto-prime, and session-prime all surface concrete recovery calls and tool-routing guidance before the user has to remember the architecture.
102989:### Finding 4: Mnemosyne’s project/global model is a thin UX alias over collection naming, not a replacement for Public’s governed scope model
102998:- **What it does**: Public’s handler and runtime hints clearly support local or global memory retrieval plus automatic community fallback, but the shared `memory_search` schema block does not expose a `retrievalLevel` input at all. Mnemosyne avoids this kind of split because its project/global distinction is visible directly in separate tools.
103031:Third pattern: offline-first readiness and failure handling. 114671:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne auto-runs `mnemosyne init` for project/global collections but never checks the subprocess exit code in those init paths; the README also says first use downloads about 500 MB of models. If init or model download fails, the wrapper does not surface a readiness state up front. Public instead validates provider config, runs integrity checks, refuses embedding-dimension mismatches, and emits explicit degraded or fatal startup guidance.
114697:### Finding 5: The compaction hook can advertise broken memory tools
114699:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne always injects its memory-usage instructions during `experimental.session.compacting`; it does not check whether the binary is missing, init failed, or the backend is unhealthy. Public’s compaction transport only injects when a transport plan is available, tracks `runtimeReady`, preserves `lastRuntimeError`, and exposes a status tool.
114726:- Questions addressed: missing-binary handling, first-model-download readiness, corruption and repair visibility, rename and scope migration risk, delete hygiene, compaction behavior under failure, wrapper-level scaling limits
114730:- What worked: reading the wrapper top-to-bottom first, then comparing it directly against Public’s startup, health, retrieval-fallback, and compaction code made the real failure boundary very clear.

114888:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
114892:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
114898:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
114900:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
114913:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
114915:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
114926:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
114927:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
114929:11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
114930:12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.

115687:OpenCode plugin for **local persistent memory** using [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne). Gives your AI coding agent memory that persists across sessions -- entirely offline, no cloud APIs.
115689:This is the local/offline alternative to cloud-based memory plugins like opencode-supermemory.

117620:The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.

121703:### Finding 1: Health-gated memory compaction guidance is the easiest high-value transfer
121705:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne appends a fixed memory-usage block during `experimental.session.compacting`; Public already has a transport-gated compaction pipeline with cache, `runtimeReady`, `lastRuntimeError`, and a status tool.
121706:- **Why it matters for us**: Integration effort is low because the hook plumbing already exists in Public. The work is mostly adding a memory-oriented compaction block to the transport plan, not changing retrieval internals.
121724:### Finding 4: `core=true` is only a viable shortcut if it maps cleanly onto Public’s constitutional-tier governance
121727:- **Why it matters for us**: Integration effort is medium-high because this is not just a UI nicety. A `core=true` alias would have to map to constitutional or critical semantics, audit history, cache invalidation, and save/update guardrails without weakening governance.
121753:- Questions addressed: top-5 adoption candidates, compaction-survival fit, startup/AGENTS discipline reuse, project/global ergonomics, `core` mapping, thin-plugin feasibility, current Public overlap with hybrid retrieval and CocoIndex
121754:- Questions answered: the best near-term transfer is a health-gated memory compaction block; startup/AGENTS guidance is also easy to absorb; project/global aliases and `core=true` are feasible but need wrapper-only treatment over existing governed surfaces; a native OpenCode memory plugin is viable only as a delegating facade. 121894:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
121898:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
121904:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
121906:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
121919:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
121921:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.

125379:### Finding 1: Adopting Mnemosyne-style compaction reminders risks duplicating Public’s existing compaction channels
125382:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne appends a fixed memory-usage instruction block during `experimental.session.compacting`, while Public already has a transport-backed compaction note plus a separate `autoSurfaceAtCompaction()` path that injects retrieved memory/context.
125383:- **Why it matters for us**: The transfer is still attractive, but the main risk is prompt collision: we could end up with three compaction surfaces saying similar things in different voices, which weakens retrieval-policy clarity and increases prompt noise.
125403:### Finding 4: A `core=true` shortcut could erode constitutional-tier discipline if it acts like a simple tag
125406:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne maps `core=true` to a `core` tag and describes it as always available context. Public’s “always surface” path is tied to the constitutional tier, token budgeting, cache keys, mutation hooks, and validated importance-tier updates.
125407:- **Why it matters for us**: The risk is semantic collapse. If `core=true` becomes a casual alias, operators will over-promote memories, inflate the constitutional budget, and create ambiguity between `constitutional`, `critical`, and merely “important” memories.
125414:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne is a tiny OpenCode plugin that delegates everything to a local binary via argv-safe `Bun.spawn`. Public already has a richer MCP server plus OpenCode transport payloads for bootstrap/resume/compaction.
125415:- **Why it matters for us**: The biggest risk is split truth. If we add a native plugin that reinterprets save/search/scope logic instead of delegating to MCP, we will create semantic drift, duplicate failure handling, and mismatched recovery behavior. The offline-first dependency model also adds first-run latency and missing-binary failure states that Public’s current MCP surface does not require.
125436:- Questions addressed: adoption risk for compaction reminders, startup/AGENTS policy reuse, project/global ergonomic aliases, `core=true` mapping, thin plugin delegation, offline-first failure handling, and separation from CocoIndex/code-graph responsibilities
125437:- Questions answered: the safest near-term adoption is still the compaction reminder pattern, but only as a deduped add-on to existing compaction transport; AGENTS guidance should be rewritten for governed saves instead of copied; project/global and `core=true` need wrapper-only prototypes because Public’s scope and constitutional semantics are stricter than Mnemosyne’s; a native OpenCode facade is viable only if it delegates to MCP and never becomes a second memory authority. Validation command run: `bash .opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/validate.sh "/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main" --strict`, which returned `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while also emitting `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.
125440:- What worked: starting from the wrapper source and then tracing Public’s save/search/compaction/governance paths made the real risks obvious; the important differences are not retrieval quality but scope authority, mutation semantics, and compaction duplication.
125444:Inspect whether Public’s OpenCode transport builder can host a dedicated memory-tool reminder block with strict dedupe and channel ownership, then separately design a read-only `project/global` alias prototype that maps onto existing `memory_search` scope rules without introducing any new write semantics.

125590:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
125594:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
125600:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
125602:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
125615:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
125617:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
125628:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
125629:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
125631:11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
125632:12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.

125696:**Finding: Memory-tool awareness survives compaction through prompt injection**

125699:- Why it matters: Public may already preserve structural state across compaction, but it does not yet remind the agent of concrete memory tools in the same direct way.

126378:OpenCode plugin for **local persistent memory** using [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne). Gives your AI coding agent memory that persists across sessions -- entirely offline, no cloud APIs.
126380:This is the local/offline alternative to cloud-based memory plugins like opencode-supermemory.
126393:See the [mnemosyne README](https://github.com/gandazgul/mnemosyne#quick-start) for detailed setup instructions. On first use, mnemosyne will automatically download its ML models (~500 MB one-time).

130466:   139	The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.

130807:I’ve got the key implementation slices now. I’m doing one last semantic pass over the repo for compaction and scope patterns so I don’t miss a better existing fit before I lock the ranking and roadmap.
130832:You are a research specialist in hybrid search architectures (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin design, memory scoping patterns, and session compaction resilience. Work like a systems analyst who can separate TypeScript wrapper behavior from backend README claims, trace plugin lifecycle and hook wiring precisely, and translate OpenCode Mnemosyne's concrete design into practical improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`.
130836:Research OpenCode Mnemosyne's hybrid search architecture, plugin design patterns, and compaction survival strategies to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around combining text and vector search, OpenCode plugin integration, project-vs-global memory scoping, and preserving memory awareness across context compaction. Focus on what the external repo actually proves in `src/index.ts` and what the README describes about the Mnemosyne backend. Classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
130842:OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
130844:The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
130857:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
130859:What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
130870:8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
130871:9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
130873:11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
130874:12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.

130938:**Finding: Memory-tool awareness survives compaction through prompt injection**
130940:- What it does: Mnemosyne appends a compact memory-usage instruction block during `experimental.session.compacting`, while Public's existing compaction plugin injects structural transport context rather than memory-tool reminders.
130941:- Why it matters: Public may already preserve structural state across compaction, but it does not yet remind the agent of concrete memory tools in the same direct way.
130943:- Affected area: compaction strategy, startup guidance, memory-tool ergonomics
130963:- project/global scoping model
130964:- documented hybrid search contract
130966:- compaction-hook behavior and comparison with Public
130967:- offline-first operational tradeoffs
130979:Rank findings in this order: compaction-resilience leverage, fit with Public's current hybrid retrieval stack, clarity of wrapper-vs-backend boundary, usefulness of project/global scoping ergonomics, offline-first operational value, implementation cost, and clean differentiation from phases `001` and `003`.
130985:- explicit comparison against current Public files for hybrid retrieval, session recovery, compaction injection, and CocoIndex positioning
131006:  - **Role** is specialized in hybrid search, plugin architecture, scoping, and compaction resilience
131010:  - **Examples** show strong Mnemosyne-derived findings about hybrid search contracts and compaction hooks
131013:- findings compare Mnemosyne with current Public hybrid retrieval and compaction behavior rather than a stale baseline
131014:- project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, offline-first tradeoffs, and compaction survival are all covered directly
131020:- the reading order started with `src/index.ts`, then `package.json`, then the five tools, then the compaction hook, then `README.md`
131038:### Finding 1: Add a transport-owned memory reminder block at compaction time
131040:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne injects an explicit “use memory tools” reminder into `experimental.session.compacting`, while Public’s compaction path currently carries continuity payloads and surfaced memories, not a direct tool-usage reminder.
131041:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the clearest gap with the best fit: it improves post-compaction memory awareness without changing retrieval math or governance. Affected subsystem: OpenCode compaction transport.
131052:### Finding 3: Read-side `project/global` aliases are worth prototyping, but only as thin wrappers over existing retrieval scope
131054:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne exposes separate project/global recall tools; Public already has `retrievalLevel: local|global|auto`, but that capability is less ergonomic than Mnemosyne’s named split.
131066:### Finding 5: A direct `core=true` shortcut should be rejected on Public’s primary surface
131075:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne proves that a very thin OpenCode plugin can be ergonomically nice, but Public already has a richer transport/MCP authority layer with startup, resume, and compaction payload planning.
131080:### Finding 7: Mnemosyne’s offline-first binary/model stack is useful as an optional pattern, not as Public’s default deployment model
131110:- Questions addressed: compaction-survival leverage, governed mutation mismatch, project/global ergonomics, AGENTS/startup reuse, `core=true` semantics, OpenCode plugin layering, offline-first tradeoffs, current hybrid-retrieval overlap, CocoIndex separation.
131111:- Questions answered: Phase A adopt now: add a deduped memory-tool reminder to the existing compaction transport, and rewrite startup/AGENTS wording around `session_bootstrap` plus governed save flows. Phase B prototype later: add read-only `project/global` recall aliases that compile down to current retrieval scope, with no write aliases. Phase C hold or reject: do not add a direct `core=true` shortcut, do not replace Public’s retrieval stack with Mnemosyne, and only pursue an OpenCode-native facade if it delegates to MCP instead of becoming a second authority.
131112:- Questions answered: Mnemosyne’s genuinely new value for Public is not hybrid ranking, it is wrapper ergonomics plus compaction-time memory awareness.
131117:- What worked: tracing `external/src/index.ts` first, then reading Public’s transport, save/delete, and constitutional paths line-by-line made the real adoption boundary obvious: ergonomics and compaction are portable, authority and mutation semantics are not.
131121:Implement the Phase A spike in design form before any runtime change: define a single transport-owned compaction reminder block with strict dedupe/ownership rules, then draft the exact Public-facing wording for “recall early, save governed memories, delete contradictions carefully” so it aligns with `session_bootstrap`, `memory_context`, `memory_save`, and `memory_delete` instead of Mnemosyne’s direct CLI mental model.

131929:    82	Mnemosyne is a local document store with hybrid search:
131977:    17	### Finding 4: A `core=true` shortcut could erode constitutional-tier discipline if it acts like a simple tag
131980:    20	- **What it does**: Mnemosyne maps `core=true` to a `core` tag and describes it as always available context. Public’s “always surface” path is tied to the constitutional tier, token budgeting, cache keys, mutation hooks, and validated importance-tier updates.
131981:    21	- **Why it matters for us**: The risk is semantic collapse. If `core=true` becomes a casual alias, operators will over-promote memories, inflate the constitutional budget, and create ambiguity between `constitutional`, `critical`, and merely “important” memories.
131988:    28	- **What it does**: Mnemosyne is a tiny OpenCode plugin that delegates everything to a local binary via argv-safe `Bun.spawn`. Public already has a richer MCP server plus OpenCode transport payloads for bootstrap/resume/compaction.
131989:    29	- **Why it matters for us**: The biggest risk is split truth. If we add a native plugin that reinterprets save/search/scope logic instead of delegating to MCP, we will create semantic drift, duplicate failure handling, and mismatched recovery behavior. The offline-first dependency model also adds first-run latency and missing-binary failure states that Public’s current MCP surface does not require.
132010:    50	- Questions addressed: adoption risk for compaction reminders, startup/AGENTS policy reuse, project/global ergonomic aliases, `core=true` mapping, thin plugin delegation, offline-first failure handling, and separation from CocoIndex/code-graph responsibilities
132011:    51	- Questions answered: the safest near-term adoption is still the compaction reminder pattern, but only as a deduped add-on to existing compaction transport; AGENTS guidance should be rewritten for governed saves instead of copied; project/global and `core=true` need wrapper-only prototypes because Public’s scope and constitutional semantics are stricter than Mnemosyne’s; a native OpenCode facade is viable only if it delegates to MCP and never becomes a second memory authority. Validation command run: `bash .opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/validate.sh "/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main" --strict`, which returned `Summary: Errors: 0  Warnings: 0` and `RESULT: PASSED`, while also emitting `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.
132014:    54	- What worked: starting from the wrapper source and then tracing Public’s save/search/compaction/governance paths made the real risks obvious; the important differences are not retrieval quality but scope authority, mutation semantics, and compaction duplication.

132035:    75	OpenCode Mnemosyne is an OpenCode plugin that wraps the Go-based Mnemosyne local memory backend. The external repo is intentionally thin: `src/index.ts` contains the plugin entry point, CLI bridge, tool registration, scope initialization, and the `experimental.session.compacting` hook, while `README.md` describes the backend's hybrid retrieval model and intended agent workflow. The documented retrieval stack combines full-text search (SQLite FTS5 with BM25 ranking) and vector search (sqlite-vec cosine similarity using `snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v1.5`), then fuses both with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. All inference is local, the ML model is downloaded on first use (~500 MB), and no cloud APIs are involved.
132037:    77	The plugin exposes five memory tools: `memory_recall`, `memory_recall_global`, `memory_store`, `memory_store_global`, and `memory_delete`. Project scope is derived from the current directory name and auto-initialized during plugin load; the global collection is initialized lazily on first global write. The wrapper shells out to the `mnemosyne` binary via `Bun.spawn`, quotes search strings to avoid FTS syntax issues, and injects compact memory-usage guidance into the compaction prompt so agents retain memory awareness after context resets.
132050:    90	`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit Memory with a hybrid vector/BM25 memory retrieval pipeline in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts`, startup and resume flows in `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts`, and CocoIndex for semantic code search through `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md`. It also already has a compaction-oriented transport plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` that injects structural context during `experimental.session.compacting`.
132052:    92	What this repo does **not** currently have is an OpenCode memory plugin that wraps a standalone local binary, a first-class project/global tool pair with Mnemosyne's lightweight ergonomics, or a memory-specific compaction hook that injects tool-usage guidance directly into the compaction prompt. Do not repeat the outdated claim that Public lacks hybrid BM25+vector memory retrieval; compare Mnemosyne against the current code, not stale assumptions.
132063:   103	8. Examine the `experimental.session.compacting` hook implementation in `src/index.ts` line by line. Treat this as the most novel feature of the phase: capture exactly what guidance is injected, when it runs, and how that compares with Public's structural compaction plugin in `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js`.
132064:   104	9. Only after the wrapper analysis, read `external/opencode-mnemosyne-main/README.md`. Use it to extract hybrid-search claims, model choice, AGENTS.md guidance, offline-first positioning, and the stated project/global collection model. Distinguish clearly between wrapper-verified behavior and backend claims that are only documented here.
132066:   106	11. Compare Mnemosyne directly against current `Code_Environment/Public` code: `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts` for existing hybrid retrieval, `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts` for session bootstrap/resume behavior, `.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js` for compaction injection, and `.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md` for semantic code-search positioning.
132067:   107	12. Resolve cross-phase boundaries explicitly. Do not redo phase `001` as generic FTS5 study or phase `003` as generic BM25 study. This phase owns wrapper-to-backend boundaries, vector-search implications, OpenCode plugin ergonomics, project/global scoping, AGENTS guidance, and memory-awareness compaction behavior.
132074:   114	    Research the external repository at /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/opencode-mnemosyne-main and identify concrete improvements for Code_Environment/Public, especially around hybrid search (BM25 + vector embeddings), OpenCode plugin architecture, memory scoping (project vs global), session compaction resilience, and offline-first memory design.

132092:   132	10. What are the practical implications of the offline-first design, including local model download cost, first-run latency, privacy benefits, and failure handling when the binary or model is missing?

132131:   171	**Finding: Memory-tool awareness survives compaction through prompt injection**
132133:   173	- What it does: Mnemosyne appends a compact memory-usage instruction block during `experimental.session.compacting`, while Public's existing compaction plugin injects structural transport context rather than memory-tool reminders.
132134:   174	- Why it matters: Public may already preserve structural state across compaction, but it does not yet remind the agent of concrete memory tools in the same direct way.

134952:   139	The two embedding models trade off quality against convenience. The local model (`all-MiniLM-L6-v2`) requires no API key and works offline, making it the right default for most projects. The Voyage Code 3 model produces 1024-dimensional vectors trained specifically on code, and consistently returns higher-quality results for complex queries on large codebases. Switching models requires a full reset and reindex because the vector dimensions are incompatible.

135529:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s wrapper forwards search requests to a backend whose BM25/vector/RRF internals are only README-documented, while Public already runs a visible multi-stage hybrid search pipeline.
135536:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne preserves memory behavior across compaction with an explicit action card, while Public currently preserves continuity/state but not equally direct memory-tool verbs on the OpenCode surface.
135537:- **Why it matters for us**: A transport-owned, deduped memory reminder would improve post-compaction behavior without changing retrieval authority.
135543:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne repeats “recall early, save after decisions, delete contradictions” in AGENTS and compaction, but Public’s save/delete paths are governed and more constrained.
135550:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne gives simple project/global tool pairs; Public already has richer runtime scoping via `retrievalLevel` and broader fallback behavior.
135569:### Finding 7: A direct `core=true` shortcut should not be added to Public’s main surface
135590:### Finding 10: Optional offline backend readiness is worth prototyping, but Mnemosyne’s runtime stack should not become Public’s default
135613:- Executive summary: 10 consolidated decisions. `adopt now`: 2. `prototype later`: 2. `NEW FEATURE`: 2. `reject`: 4. The highest-confidence transfers are compaction-time memory action guidance and rewritten startup policy; the highest-confidence rejections are basename-derived scope, `core=true`, and Mnemosyne-style mutation semantics.
135615:- Questions addressed: hybrid contract vs wrapper proof, compaction resilience, AGENTS/startup reuse, project/global ergonomics, scope identity stability, inline save ergonomics, constitutional pinning semantics, mutation/audit model, OpenCode facade design, federated retrieval separation, offline readiness tradeoffs
135616:- Questions answered: Public should borrow Mnemosyne’s behavioral ergonomics, not its authority model. The safe path is `session_bootstrap`-aligned guidance plus a transport-owned compaction memory card, with any future aliases or facades compiled into existing MCP/governance surfaces.
135624:Design two packet-ready follow-ons before any runtime edits: one for a deduped OpenCode compaction memory action card owned by transport, and one for a governed `memory_remember` / read-only `project|global` facade that delegates entirely to existing MCP scope and save authority.
135639:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s wrapper forwards search requests to a backend whose BM25/vector/RRF internals are only README-documented, while Public already runs a visible multi-stage hybrid search pipeline.
135646:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne preserves memory behavior across compaction with an explicit action card, while Public currently preserves continuity/state but not equally direct memory-tool verbs on the OpenCode surface.
135647:- **Why it matters for us**: A transport-owned, deduped memory reminder would improve post-compaction behavior without changing retrieval authority.
135653:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne repeats “recall early, save after decisions, delete contradictions” in AGENTS and compaction, but Public’s save/delete paths are governed and more constrained.
135660:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne gives simple project/global tool pairs; Public already has richer runtime scoping via `retrievalLevel` and broader fallback behavior.
135679:### Finding 7: A direct `core=true` shortcut should not be added to Public’s main surface
135700:### Finding 10: Optional offline backend readiness is worth prototyping, but Mnemosyne’s runtime stack should not become Public’s default
135723:- Executive summary: 10 consolidated decisions. `adopt now`: 2. `prototype later`: 2. `NEW FEATURE`: 2. `reject`: 4. The highest-confidence transfers are compaction-time memory action guidance and rewritten startup policy; the highest-confidence rejections are basename-derived scope, `core=true`, and Mnemosyne-style mutation semantics.
135725:- Questions addressed: hybrid contract vs wrapper proof, compaction resilience, AGENTS/startup reuse, project/global ergonomics, scope identity stability, inline save ergonomics, constitutional pinning semantics, mutation/audit model, OpenCode facade design, federated retrieval separation, offline readiness tradeoffs
135726:- Questions answered: Public should borrow Mnemosyne’s behavioral ergonomics, not its authority model. The safe path is `session_bootstrap`-aligned guidance plus a transport-owned compaction memory card, with any future aliases or facades compiled into existing MCP/governance surfaces.
135734:Design two packet-ready follow-ons before any runtime edits: one for a deduped OpenCode compaction memory action card owned by transport, and one for a governed `memory_remember` / read-only `project|global` facade that delegates entirely to existing MCP scope and save authority.

# Iteration 021: CROSS-SYSTEM PATTERNS

## Focus
CROSS-SYSTEM PATTERNS: Compare patterns from this system against the other 4 research phases. What converges across all systems? What's unique to this one?

## Findings
### Finding 1: The strongest convergence is behavioral continuity at runtime seams, not better ranking math
- **Source**: [Mnemosyne wrapper]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L208), [Public compaction plugin]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js#L396), [Engram iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/research/iterations/iteration-001.md#L15713), [Mex iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md#L9303), [Modus iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/research/iterations/iteration-013.md#L36), [MemPalace iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/research/iterations/iteration-017.md#L5411)
- **What it does**: Across all five systems, the durable value shows up at startup, resume, compaction, save timing, or repair timing. Engram uses hook/setup injection, Mex uses a control-plane loop, Modus pushes toward execution-readiness, MemPalace emphasizes pre-compaction preservation, and Mnemosyne injects a direct memory action card during `experimental.session.compacting`.
- **Why it matters**: The cross-system consensus is that the next leverage point for Public is agent behavior under context loss or drift, not another retrieval channel.
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 2: Every serious system grows a control plane above raw storage, but Mnemosyne is the thinnest version of that pattern
- **Source**: [Engram iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/research/iterations/iteration-001.md#L15692), [Mex iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md#L9289), [Modus iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/research/iterations/iteration-013.md#L8), [MemPalace iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/research/iterations/iteration-017.md#L5418), [Mnemosyne README]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L62)
- **What it does**: Engram has explicit session objects, Mex has a truth-audit and repair loop, Modus has governance/proposal/readiness planes, and MemPalace has protocol/status surfaces. Mnemosyne only adds AGENTS guidance plus a compaction reminder; it does not add a richer operator-facing control plane.
- **Why it matters**: This makes Mnemosyne useful as a minimum viable behavioral layer, but not as the architectural ceiling. Public’s gap is broader: it still lacks a dedicated stale-truth/remediation plane more than it lacks retrieval features.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 3: Scope separation converges, but Mnemosyne’s project/global split is uniquely simple and uniquely weak
- **Source**: [Mnemosyne index.ts]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L27), [Mnemosyne README]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L51), [Modus iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/research/iterations/iteration-013.md#L8), [MemPalace iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/research/iterations/iteration-017.md#L5439), [Public memory search]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts#L14)
- **What it does**: All systems partition memory somehow: Engram by agent/admin and sessions, Mex by scaffold vs repair plane, Modus by separate planes, MemPalace by layers and fact plane. Mnemosyne is the simplest: basename-derived project collection, a single global collection, plus `core` tagging.
- **Why it matters**: The ergonomic lesson is real, but the identity model is brittle. Mnemosyne’s split is useful as a UX alias, not as a storage/governance model for Public.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 4: Mnemosyne is uniquely valuable as a thin OpenCode-native facade over a local backend, not as a retrieval-engine replacement
- **Source**: [Mnemosyne package]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/package.json#L1), [Mnemosyne README]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L80), [Public startup]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts#L1495), [Public compaction plugin]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js#L296), [CocoIndex README]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/mcp-coco-index/README.md#L42)
- **What it does**: The checked-out Mnemosyne repo is mostly a Bun/OpenCode wrapper plus README contract. Its BM25/vector/RRF story is documented, but the wrapper itself mainly contributes CLI bridging, tool exposure, and compaction guidance. Public already has visible hybrid search and separate semantic code search.
- **Why it matters**: Cross-system, the unique contribution here is facade ergonomics: an easy local memory plugin with a tight OpenCode surface. It is not evidence for reworking Public’s retrieval core.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 5: Readiness and trust boundaries are a hidden convergence theme, and Mnemosyne is uniquely brittle there
- **Source**: [Mnemosyne index.ts]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L78), [Mnemosyne README]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L18), [Public startup]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts#L1589), [Mex iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md#L9310), [Modus iteration]( /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/research/iterations/iteration-013.md#L15)
- **What it does**: The other systems all expose some visible posture around truth, trust, freshness, or readiness. Mnemosyne auto-inits collections and depends on a first-use model download, but the wrapper does not fail closed on init readiness and can still advertise memory usage at compaction even if the backend is broken.
- **Why it matters**: This is the biggest unique warning from this system. If we borrow its ergonomics, we should also invert its failure posture and keep readiness explicit and health-gated.
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

## Assessment
- New information ratio: 0.33

## Recommended Next Focus
Design one Public-native packet around a unified memory operating protocol: transport-owned compaction guidance plus health-gated readiness and a bounded stale-truth repair loop. Keep retrieval math explicitly out of scope for that next pass.
